name: Security
description: From phishing to pass-the-hash — security fundamentals, application security and operations. 60-question seed set, growing to 300.

questions.text: What is phishing?
options: Encrypting a victim's files and then demanding a ransom payment
Tricking people into revealing information via fake messages
Guessing passwords with a wordlist
Scanning a target network for open and vulnerable ports
questions.explanation: Phishing attacks the human, not the machine: a convincing fake email, site or message that harvests credentials or data.

questions.text: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) means…
options: It skips locking altogether and therefore never blocks a caller
It caches the entire map inside the CPU's fast registers
It is fully single-threaded and serialises every method call
Proving identity with two different kinds of evidence
questions.explanation: Something you know, have, or are — two different categories. Two passwords are still one factor.

questions.text: In the CIA triad, the three properties are…
options: Confidentiality, inspection, authorisation
Control, identity, authentication
Certification, integrity, accountability
Confidentiality, integrity, availability
questions.explanation: Keep data secret, keep it unaltered, keep it reachable — every control maps to at least one of the three.

questions.text: The main job of a firewall is to…
options: Remove viruses that are already on a machine
Back up critical files to a safe location
Encrypt all traffic leaving the network
Allow or block network traffic based on rules
questions.explanation: A firewall is a traffic filter. It doesn't clean infections or encrypt data — it decides what may pass.

questions.text: Ransomware typically…
options: Silently mines cryptocurrency in the background for the attacker
Quietly records absolutely everything you type on the keyboard
Displays streams of unwanted advertising inside the browser
Encrypts your files and demands payment for the key
questions.explanation: Encrypt, extort, sometimes also exfiltrate and threaten to leak. Backups tested offline are the real defence.

questions.text: Social engineering is best described as…
options: Writing a piece of malware inside a social media platform
Analysing social networks to fully map out an organisation
Manipulating people into breaking security procedures
Building engineering teams that keep security front of mind
questions.explanation: It targets trust and habit rather than software: pretexts, urgency, authority, and fear all feature.

questions.text: The most practical way to have strong, unique passwords everywhere is…
options: A password manager generating and storing them
Writing them in a notebook kept next to the PC
A memorable pattern that changes per site name
One very strong password reused on every site
questions.explanation: Managers make unique, random passwords free. Patterns are guessable once one site leaks; reuse turns one breach into many.

questions.text: The padlock icon in the browser address bar means…
options: The website is safe and can be fully trusted
The connection to the site is encrypted
The site has been scanned and has no malware
The site owner's identity has been police-checked
questions.explanation: It only says the transport is encrypted. Phishing sites use HTTPS too — the padlock is not a trust badge.

questions.text: Installing software updates promptly matters mainly because updates…
options: Free up disk space used by older versions
Are required to keep the software licence valid
Patch security holes attackers actively exploit
Make the computer run noticeably faster
questions.explanation: Most successful attacks use known, already-patched vulnerabilities. Updating closes the door before someone walks in.

questions.text: The principle of least privilege says…
options: Give administrators the fewest possible accounts
Users with less experience get less bandwidth
Give each account only the access its job needs
Privileged accounts should log in least often
questions.explanation: Smaller permissions mean smaller blast radius when an account is compromised — and most eventually are.

questions.text: The 3-2-1 backup rule is…
options: Back up 3 times a day, keep 2 days, delete after 1
3 drives mirrored across 2 servers in 1 room
3 copies, on 2 different media, 1 kept off-site
3 people approve, 2 restore tests, 1 owner
questions.explanation: Three copies, two kinds of storage, one away from the building — so no single event (or ransomware run) takes them all.

questions.text: On public Wi-Fi, the sensible behaviour is to…
options: Disable the firewall so the captive portal loads
Share files only with devices on the same network
Use HTTP sites since HTTPS is slower on Wi-Fi
Avoid sensitive logins or use a trusted VPN
questions.explanation: Treat the network as hostile: anyone on it can try to interfere. Encryption end-to-end (HTTPS/VPN) is what protects you.

questions.text: You find a USB stick in the car park. The right move is…
options: Hand it to IT/security without plugging it in anywhere
Plug it into an offline laptop to identify the owner
Plug it in but only open files that look harmless
Format it first, then reuse it as a spare drive
questions.explanation: Dropped drives are a classic attack. 'Offline' doesn't help — malicious firmware can act as a keyboard the moment it's inserted.

questions.text: Which second factor is generally the strongest of these?
options: A memorable question about the name of your very first pet
A code from an authenticator app or hardware key
A second password that is chosen for you by the IT department
A numeric code delivered to your phone by SMS text message
questions.explanation: SMS can be intercepted or SIM-swapped; security questions are guessable. App codes and hardware keys resist both.

questions.text: "Shoulder surfing" refers to…
options: Riding on another user's open network session
Browsing over a colleague's shared drive
Following an employee through a secure door
Watching someone type credentials or read data
questions.explanation: Low-tech, effective: screens and keyboards leak. Privacy filters and awareness in public spaces are the counter.

questions.text: Antivirus software on its own…
options: Protects every device connected to the network
Helps against known malware but misses much else
Guarantees a machine cannot be infected at all
Replaces the need to install security updates
questions.explanation: AV is one layer. Novel malware, phishing, and stolen credentials sail past it — hence defence in depth.

questions.text: Spear phishing differs from ordinary phishing because it…
options: Always carries a malicious file attachment
Is sent only through social media platforms
Targets a specific person with tailored detail
Comes exclusively from compromised accounts
questions.explanation: Research makes it convincing: your name, your project, your supplier. Volume drops, success rate soars.

questions.text: "Vishing" and "smishing" are phishing carried out via…
options: Voice calls and SMS text messages
Video calls and screen sharing sessions
Voicemail transcripts and email signatures
Virtual machines and simulated environments
questions.explanation: Same con, different channel. A phone call impersonating IT support is vishing; the fake delivery text is smishing.

questions.text: "Encryption in transit" vs "encryption at rest" protect data…
options: During routine backups versus during normal everyday use
While moving over networks vs while stored on disk
While held in memory versus while inside the processor cache
Inside the cloud versus on machines physically in the office
questions.explanation: Two different exposures: interception on the wire, and theft of the disk or bucket. Serious systems need both.

questions.text: A vulnerability and an exploit are, respectively…
options: A hardware fault and the software patch for it
A misconfiguration and the person who finds it
An attack in progress and the tool that stops it
A weakness in a system and code that abuses it
questions.explanation: The vulnerability is the unlocked window; the exploit is the technique for climbing through it.

questions.text: The standard defence against SQL injection is…
options: Renaming all the tables so that attackers cannot guess them
Parameterised queries instead of string concatenation
Hiding detailed database error messages away from end users
Blocking the word SELECT anywhere in user input fields
questions.explanation: Parameters keep data and SQL separate, so input can never become code. Filtering keywords is a losing game.

questions.text: A successful stored XSS attack lets the attacker…
options: Run their JavaScript in every victim's browser
Read arbitrary files from the web server's disk
Overload the server until it stops responding
Bypass the database's authentication entirely
questions.explanation: The payload persists in the app and executes in visitors' browsers — stealing sessions, redressing pages, riding logged-in users.

questions.text: CSRF (cross-site request forgery) tricks…
options: A logged-in user's browser into sending unwanted requests
A certificate authority into signing a rogue, unauthorised cert
The server into issuing outbound requests to an attacker's site
The DNS resolver into caching a deliberately forged record
questions.explanation: The browser helpfully attaches cookies to requests it's told to make. Anti-CSRF tokens and SameSite cookies break the trick.

questions.text: The key difference between hashing and encryption is that hashing…
options: Works only on passwords rather than files
Uses much longer keys than encryption does
Is one-way and not meant to be reversed
Is faster but produces far larger output
questions.explanation: Encryption round-trips with a key; a hash is a fingerprint. That's why passwords are hashed, never encrypted.

questions.text: Salting password hashes defends against…
options: Precomputed tables and identical-hash matching
Timing attacks on the login comparison code
Users choosing the same password twice in a row
Attackers reading the database in the first place
questions.explanation: A unique salt per user makes rainbow tables useless and hides who shares the same password.

questions.text: A TLS certificate fundamentally proves that…
options: The site contains no malicious content at all
The traffic cannot be logged by intermediaries
A trusted CA vouches for the domain's identity
The server passed a recent penetration test
questions.explanation: The chain of signatures ties the public key to the domain via a CA your device already trusts. Nothing more, nothing less.

questions.text: The OWASP Top 10 is…
options: A ranked list of the most common web application risks
The ten most dangerous known malware families in circulation
Ten mandatory controls required for ISO 27001 certification
A published benchmark of the ten most secure web frameworks
questions.explanation: A community-maintained awareness list — injection, broken access control, misconfig and friends. A shared vocabulary for appsec.

questions.text: The difference between an IDS and an IPS is that an IPS…
options: Uses signatures while an IDS uses anomaly detection
Works on hosts while an IDS works on networks
Is a hardware device while an IDS is software
Can actively block traffic, not just raise alerts
questions.explanation: Detection vs prevention: inline placement lets an IPS drop the packet, at the cost of false positives blocking real traffic.

questions.text: A DMZ in network design is…
options: The single segment where no network traffic is ever allowed
A buffer zone for services exposed to the internet
The internal zone reserved exclusively for administrators
A honeypot network deliberately built to attract attackers
questions.explanation: Public-facing servers live there so a compromise doesn't land directly inside the internal network.

questions.text: An attacker running a port scan is trying to…
options: Exhaust the target's available connections
Crack the encryption keys of open sessions
Redirect the target's traffic to their machine
Map which services the target exposes
questions.explanation: Reconnaissance: open ports reveal software and versions, which point at candidate vulnerabilities.

questions.text: DNS cache poisoning lets an attacker…
options: Register expired domains before the owner
Send victims to attacker-controlled addresses
Take the DNS service offline for everyone
Read all queries made through the resolver
questions.explanation: Corrupt the name-to-address mapping and every 'correct' URL quietly resolves to the wrong server.

questions.text: The practical defence against man-in-the-middle attacks is…
options: Using long and random session identifiers
Hiding the network name so it can't be found
Rotating passwords on a fixed schedule
End-to-end encryption with verified identities
questions.explanation: If traffic is encrypted and endpoints authenticate each other (TLS + cert validation), sitting in the middle yields nothing usable.

questions.text: EDR differs from classic antivirus mainly because it…
options: It runs entirely in the cloud instead of on the local endpoint
Records endpoint behaviour for detection and response
It is updated once a month rather than on a continuous basis
It concentrates on scanning email rather than files on disk
questions.explanation: EDR watches processes, connections and changes over time — enabling hunting and rollback, not just signature matching.

questions.text: "Lateral movement" in an intrusion means the attacker…
options: Switches to attacking a different organisation
Downgrades the connection to a weaker cipher
Moves data out of the network in small pieces
Spreads from the first foothold to other systems
questions.explanation: Initial access is rarely the target. Harvested credentials and trust relationships carry the attacker toward the crown jewels.

questions.text: Privilege escalation is when an attacker…
options: Escalates a support ticket to the admin team
Creates a large number of unprivileged accounts
Gains higher permissions than they started with
Convinces a user to approve an MFA prompt
questions.explanation: From user to root/admin via kernel bugs, misconfigurations, or stolen tokens — usually the pivotal step of a breach.

questions.text: Credential stuffing differs from brute force because it…
options: It methodically tries every character combination in order
Replays username/password pairs leaked elsewhere
It only ever works when MFA has already been fully enabled
It targets automated service accounts rather than real people
questions.explanation: It bets on reuse: billions of leaked pairs tried at scale. Unique passwords per site kill it dead.

questions.text: A CVE identifier refers to…
options: The patch that fixes a known security issue
A publicly catalogued, specific vulnerability
A certificate used for vulnerability scanning
The severity score assigned to a bug report
questions.explanation: One ID per vulnerability (e.g. CVE-2021-44228), so vendors, scanners and news all point at the same thing.

questions.text: A CVSS score of 9.8 tells you the vulnerability is…
options: Actively exploited in the wild right now
Present in your systems and reachable
Rated critical by its technical characteristics
Impossible to mitigate without a full rebuild
questions.explanation: CVSS rates theoretical severity, not your exposure. Whether it matters to you still requires asset and reachability context.

questions.text: The Content-Security-Policy header mainly limits…
options: Which sources the browser may load and run
How long responses may be cached by proxies
Which countries may reach the web application
How many requests a client may send per minute
questions.explanation: CSP is an allow-list for scripts, styles and frames — a strong second line against XSS when inline script is banned.

questions.text: "Defence in depth" means…
options: Hiring a dedicated in-house security team
Layered controls so one failure isn't fatal
Encrypting data multiple times with different keys
Placing every defence at the network perimeter
questions.explanation: Assume each layer will eventually fail. Firewalls, patching, MFA, monitoring and backups all covering for each other.

questions.text: In a pass-the-hash attack, the attacker…
options: Collides two different passwords into one identical hash value
Overwrites the password hash that is stored on the server
Authenticates using the stolen hash without cracking it
Forces the server to hash using a deliberately weaker algorithm
questions.explanation: NTLM-style protocols prove knowledge of the hash itself, so the hash is as good as the password. Cracking optional.

questions.text: Server-side request forgery (SSRF) abuses…
options: The database engine's support for calling external functions
The CDN's caching of authenticated per-user responses
The server's ability to fetch URLs on the attacker's behalf
The browser's automatic willingness to follow HTTP redirects
questions.explanation: The server sits inside the trust boundary: making it issue requests reaches internal services the attacker never could directly.

questions.text: Insecure deserialization is dangerous because untrusted input can…
options: Corrupt the application's session storage
Exhaust memory by nesting objects deeply
Overflow the parser's fixed-size input buffer
Instantiate objects that execute attacker logic
questions.explanation: Deserializers can reconstruct arbitrary object graphs; gadget chains turn that into remote code execution.

questions.text: An XXE attack exploits XML parsers that…
options: Validate against remote schemas over HTTPS
Resolve external entities defined in the document
Allow attributes longer than the specification
Accept documents without a closing root tag
questions.explanation: External entities let a document say 'include this file/URL' — leaking local files or pivoting to SSRF. Disable them.

questions.text: DNS rebinding lets a malicious page…
options: Silently downgrade DNS-over-HTTPS back to plain-text queries
Reach the victim's internal network from their browser
Register lookalike domains automatically
Poison the ISP's shared resolver cache for all other users
questions.explanation: Flip the domain's IP to an internal address after the page loads, and same-origin policy now 'permits' requests to it.

questions.text: The HSTS header exists to…
options: Pin the exact certificate the site must present
Tell proxies never to cache sensitive pages
Enable TLS session resumption across visits
Force browsers to use HTTPS for future visits
questions.explanation: It removes the plain-HTTP first hop that SSL-stripping attacks depend on; preload lists remove even the first visit.

questions.text: OCSP stapling improves revocation checking by…
options: Having the server present a signed freshness proof
Publishing revocations in the DNS zone instead
Letting browsers skip the check on fast networks
Requiring CAs to reissue certificates weekly
questions.explanation: The server fetches and 'staples' the CA-signed OCSP response, so clients don't leak visits to the CA or fail open.

questions.text: Certificate Transparency logs are valuable because they…
options: Make every issued certificate publicly auditable
Prevent CAs from issuing wildcard certificates
Encrypt the certificate chain during issuance
Speed up TLS handshakes with cached chains
questions.explanation: Domain owners can monitor the logs and spot mis-issued or rogue certificates for their names within hours.

questions.text: FIDO2/WebAuthn resists phishing because the credential…
options: Is bound to the real site's origin and won't sign elsewhere
Because it demands biometric confirmation on every single use
Because it rotates automatically after each successful login
Because it is stored inside a tamper-proof hardware security chip
questions.explanation: The authenticator signs a challenge tied to the origin: a lookalike domain gets a useless signature. Hardware and biometrics help, but origin binding is the phishing killer.

questions.text: In OAuth device-code phishing, the attacker…
options: Gets the victim to approve the attacker's device login
Replays a long-expired refresh token against the target API
Steals the client secret straight from a public code repository
Intercepts the OAuth redirect URI using a rogue application
questions.explanation: The attacker starts a device-code flow and social-engineers the victim into entering the code — no credentials ever stolen, yet the attacker's session is approved.

questions.text: A Kerberos "golden ticket" gives the attacker…
options: Self-forged tickets for any user, long after the breach
Access strictly limited to the KRBTGT service account only
A complete copy of the domain controller's entire database
The plaintext passwords of every single user in the domain
questions.explanation: With the KRBTGT hash, the attacker mints TGTs at will. Recovery means rotating that key twice — painful and often skipped.

questions.text: Kerberoasting works by…
options: Stealing tickets from memory on the domain controller
Downgrading authentication from Kerberos to NTLM
Requesting service tickets and cracking them offline
Flooding the KDC until it reuses session keys
questions.explanation: Any domain user can request tickets for service accounts; the ticket is encrypted with the service's password hash — crackable offline if the password is weak.

questions.text: A TOCTOU vulnerability arises when…
options: State changes between a check and the use of its result
Timestamps being compared across two different time zones
A token is checked before its signature is verified
Two separate threads writing to the same file simultaneously
questions.explanation: Time-of-check to time-of-use: the file you validated is swapped before you open it. Fix by making check-and-use atomic.

questions.text: Spectre-class attacks extract secrets by exploiting…
options: Voltage fluctuations measurable on the mainboard
Undocumented debug instructions in the CPU
Speculative execution leaving traces in the cache
Faulty error correction in high-density memory
questions.explanation: The CPU speculatively runs code it later discards — but cache side effects survive and can be measured to reconstruct data.

questions.text: Container escape is a critical risk primarily because containers…
options: Share the host kernel rather than being isolated VMs
Because they expose the Docker control socket to every workload
Because containers cannot apply security patches while running
Because containers always run their main process as the root user
questions.explanation: One kernel bug away from the host — and every other container on it. Root-by-default and mounted sockets make it worse, but the shared kernel is the structural reason.

questions.text: Seccomp profiles and dropped capabilities harden a container by…
options: Encrypting all of the container's filesystem layers at rest
Shrinking the kernel attack surface it can reach
Relocating the container into a separate dedicated virtual machine
Blocking every bit of the container's outbound network traffic
questions.explanation: Fewer syscalls and privileges mean fewer routes from a compromised process to the kernel. Least privilege, applied to the runtime.

questions.text: Secret scanning in CI (pre-commit hooks, pipeline scanners) exists because…
options: Encrypted repositories cannot hold API tokens
Developers cannot be given production credentials
Compliance forbids storing hashes in repositories
Committed secrets spread to every clone and backup
questions.explanation: Once a credential lands in history it's effectively public: forks, caches, CI logs. Catch it before the commit, and rotate anything that slips through.

questions.text: BGP hijacking allows an attacker to…
options: Take over the routers of a target organisation
Break TLS by compromising a certificate authority
Attract traffic for IP ranges they don't own
Forge DNS responses for entire top-level domains
questions.explanation: Announce someone else's prefixes and the internet routes their traffic to you — for interception or blackholing. RPKI is the slow-moving fix.

questions.text: DNSSEC protects…
options: Resolvers from being overwhelmed by floods of DNS queries
The full confidentiality of every DNS query that you make
Domain owners from accidentally losing their registration
The authenticity of DNS answers, not their privacy
questions.explanation: Signed records prove the answer wasn't forged — but anyone watching still sees what you looked up. Privacy needs DoH/DoT.

questions.text: In a session fixation attack, the attacker…
options: Steals the active session cookie via injected malicious JavaScript
Freezes the victim's session so that it can never expire on them
Plants a known session ID the victim then authenticates
Brute-forces session IDs until one is valid
questions.explanation: The attacker sets the ID before login; if the app doesn't regenerate it on authentication, the attacker now shares the logged-in session.
